question:
2. A real valued continuous function satisfies for all real \( x \) and \( y \) the functional equation
\[
f\left(\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}\right)=f(x) f(y)
\]

Prove that
\[
f(x)=[f(1)]^{x^{2}}
\]
solution:
Solution. A slight qualification in the statement of the problem is needed since the real valued continuous function \( f(x) \equiv 0 \) satisfies the functional equation for all real \( x \) and \( y \), but does not satisfy the relation \( f(0)= \) \( [f(1)]^{0} \), since \( 0^{0} \) is undefined.
Assume then that for some \( y_{0}, f\left(y_{0}\right) \neq 0 \). Since
\[
f(x) f\left(y_{0}\right)=f\left(\sqrt{x^{2}+y_{0}{ }^{2}}\right)=f(-x) f\left(y_{0}\right),
\]
we have \( f(x)=f(-x)=f(|x|) \) for all \( x \). We now show by induction that for any positive integer \( n \) and any real number \( x \), we have
\[
f(\sqrt{n} x)=[f(x)]^{n} .
\]

This is certainly true for \( n=1 \), and assuming it true for \( n=k \) we have
\[
\begin{array}{c}
f(\sqrt{k+1} x)=f(\sqrt{k+1}|x|)=f\left(\sqrt{(\sqrt{k} x)^{2}+x^{2}}\right)=f(\sqrt{k} x) f(x) \\
=[f(x)]^{k} f(x)=[f(x)]^{k+1} .
\end{array}
\]

Therefore (1) is true for all positive integers \( n \).
If \( p \) and \( q \) are non-zero integers, then
\[
f(p)=f(|p|)=f\left(\sqrt{p^{2}} \cdot 1\right)=[f(1)] p^{2}
\]
and
\[
f(|p|)=f\left(\sqrt{q^{2}}\left|\frac{p}{q}\right|\right)=\left[f\left(\left|\frac{p}{q}\right|\right)\right]^{q^{2}} .
\]

From these two relations it follows that
\[
\left[f\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)\right]^{q^{2}}=[f(1)]^{p^{2}} .
\]

If \( f(1)>0 \) then it follows that
\[
\left[f\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)\right]=[f(1)]^{p^{2} / q^{2}} ;
\]
that is, the required equation is valid for all rational values of \( x \) except, perhaps, \( x=0 \). By continuity it follows for all values of \( x \).

If \( f(1)=0 \), then (2) implies that \( f(p / q)=0 \) for all non-zero integers \( p \) and \( q \), and thus \( f(x)=0 \) for all rational \( x \), hence for all real \( x \).

Finally we show that \( f(1)<0 \) is impossible. If \( p \) is even and \( q \) is odd, equation (2) implies that \( f(p / q)>0 \). Hence \( f(x)>0 \) for a dense set of \( x \), and therefore \( f(x) \geq 0 \) for all \( x \); in particular, \( f(1) \geq 0 \).

Remark. If we consider the function \( g \) defined by
\[
g(x)=\log f(\sqrt{x}) \quad \text { for } x \geq 0,
\]
then \( g \) satisfies the famous Cauchy functional equation
\[
g(x+y)=g(x)+g(y)
\]
whose only continuous solution is
\[
g(x)=g(1) x
\]
and it readily follows that \( f(x)=f(1)^{x^{2}} \). This argument requires showing first that (3) defines a function; i.e., that \( f(\sqrt{x})>0 \) for \( x \geq 0 \), which is true unless \( f \) vanishes identically.

descriptive_long.question:
2. A real valued continuous function satisfies for all real \( variablex \) and \( variabley \) the functional equation
\[
functionf\left(\sqrt{variablex^{2}+variabley^{2}}\right)=functionf(variablex) functionf(variabley)
\]

Prove that
\[
functionf(variablex)=[functionf(1)]^{variablex^{2}}
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. A slight qualification in the statement of the problem is needed since the real valued continuous function \( functionf(variablex) \equiv 0 \) satisfies the functional equation for all real \( variablex \) and \( variabley \), but does not satisfy the relation \( functionf(0)= \) \( [functionf(1)]^{0} \), since \( 0^{0} \) is undefined.
Assume then that for some \( constyzero, functionf\left(constyzero\right) \neq 0 \). Since
\[
functionf(variablex) functionf\left(constyzero\right)=functionf\left(\sqrt{variablex^{2}+constyzero^{2}}\right)=functionf(-variablex) functionf\left(constyzero\right),
\]
we have \( functionf(variablex)=functionf(-variablex)=functionf(|variablex|) \) for all \( variablex \). We now show by induction that for any positive integer \( integern \) and any real number \( variablex \), we have
\[
functionf(\sqrt{integern}\, variablex)=[functionf(variablex)]^{integern} .
\]

This is certainly true for \( integern=1 \), and assuming it true for \( integern=k \) we have
\[
\begin{array}{c}
functionf(\sqrt{k+1}\, variablex)=functionf(\sqrt{k+1}|variablex|)=functionf\left(\sqrt{(\sqrt{k}\, variablex)^{2}+variablex^{2}}\right)=functionf(\sqrt{k}\, variablex) functionf(variablex) \\
=[functionf(variablex)]^{k} functionf(variablex)=[functionf(variablex)]^{k+1} .
\end{array}
\]

Therefore (1) is true for all positive integers \( integern \).
If \( integerp \) and \( integerq \) are non-zero integers, then
\[
functionf(integerp)=functionf(|integerp|)=functionf\left(\sqrt{integerp^{2}} \cdot 1\right)=[functionf(1)]^{integerp^{2}}
\]
and
\[
functionf(|integerp|)=functionf\left(\sqrt{integerq^{2}}\left|\frac{integerp}{integerq}\right|\right)=\left[functionf\left(\left|\frac{integerp}{integerq}\right|\right)\right]^{integerq^{2}} .
\]

From these two relations it follows that
\[
\left[functionf\left(\frac{integerp}{integerq}\right)\right]^{integerq^{2}}=[functionf(1)]^{integerp^{2}} .
\]

If \( functionf(1)>0 \) then it follows that
\[
\left[functionf\left(\frac{integerp}{integerq}\right)\right]=[functionf(1)]^{integerp^{2} / integerq^{2}} ;
\]
that is, the required equation is valid for all rational values of \( variablex \) except, perhaps, \( variablex=0 \). By continuity it follows for all values of \( variablex \).

If \( functionf(1)=0 \), then (2) implies that \( functionf(integerp / integerq)=0 \) for all non-zero integers \( integerp \) and \( integerq \), and thus \( functionf(variablex)=0 \) for all rational \( variablex \), hence for all real \( variablex \).

Finally we show that \( functionf(1)<0 \) is impossible. If \( integerp \) is even and \( integerq \) is odd, equation (2) implies that \( functionf(integerp / integerq)>0 \). Hence \( functionf(variablex)>0 \) for a dense set of \( variablex \), and therefore \( functionf(variablex) \geq 0 \) for all \( variablex \); in particular, \( functionf(1) \geq 0 \).

Remark. If we consider the function \( functiong \) defined by
\[
functiong(variablex)=\log functionf(\sqrt{variablex}) \quad \text { for } variablex \geq 0,
\]
then \( functiong \) satisfies the famous Cauchy functional equation
\[
functiong(variablex+variabley)=functiong(variablex)+functiong(variabley)
\]
whose only continuous solution is
\[
functiong(variablex)=functiong(1) variablex
\]
and it readily follows that \( functionf(variablex)=functionf(1)^{variablex^{2}} \). This argument requires showing first that (3) defines a function; i.e., that \( functionf(\sqrt{variablex})>0 \) for \( variablex \geq 0 \), which is true unless \( functionf \) vanishes identically.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
2. A real valued continuous function satisfies for all real \( tangerine \) and \( blueberry \) the functional equation
\[
watermelon\left(\sqrt{tangerine^{2}+blueberry^{2}}\right)=watermelon(tangerine) watermelon(blueberry)
\]

Prove that
\[
watermelon(tangerine)=[watermelon(1)]^{tangerine^{2}}
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. A slight qualification in the statement of the problem is needed since the real valued continuous function \( watermelon(tangerine) \equiv 0 \) satisfies the functional equation for all real \( tangerine \) and \( blueberry \), but does not satisfy the relation \( watermelon(0)= \) \( [watermelon(1)]^{0} \), since \( 0^{0} \) is undefined.
Assume then that for some \( strawberry, watermelon\left(strawberry\right) \neq 0 \). Since
\[
watermelon(tangerine) watermelon\left(strawberry\right)=watermelon\left(\sqrt{tangerine^{2}+strawberry{ }^{2}}\right)=watermelon(-tangerine) watermelon\left(strawberry\right),
\]
we have \( watermelon(tangerine)=watermelon(-tangerine)=watermelon(|tangerine|) \) for all \( tangerine \). We now show by induction that for any positive integer \( pineapple \) and any real number \( tangerine \), we have
\[
watermelon(\sqrt{pineapple} \, tangerine)=[watermelon(tangerine)]^{pineapple} .
\]

This is certainly true for \( pineapple=1 \), and assuming it true for \( pineapple=k \) we have
\[
\begin{array}{c}
watermelon(\sqrt{k+1} \, tangerine)=watermelon(\sqrt{k+1}|tangerine|)=watermelon\left(\sqrt{(\sqrt{k} \, tangerine)^{2}+tangerine^{2}}\right)=watermelon(\sqrt{k} \, tangerine) watermelon(tangerine) \\
=[watermelon(tangerine)]^{k} watermelon(tangerine)=[watermelon(tangerine)]^{k+1} .
\end{array}
\]

Therefore (1) is true for all positive integers \( pineapple \).
If \( cucumber \) and \( raspberry \) are non-zero integers, then
\[
watermelon(cucumber)=watermelon(|cucumber|)=watermelon\left(\sqrt{cucumber^{2}} \cdot 1\right)=[watermelon(1)] cucumber^{2}
\]
and
\[
watermelon(|cucumber|)=watermelon\left(\sqrt{raspberry^{2}}\left|\frac{cucumber}{raspberry}\right|\right)=\left[watermelon\left(\left|\frac{cucumber}{raspberry}\right|\right)\right]^{raspberry^{2}} .
\]

From these two relations it follows that
\[
\left[watermelon\left(\frac{cucumber}{raspberry}\right)\right]^{raspberry^{2}}=[watermelon(1)]^{cucumber^{2}} .
\]

If \( watermelon(1)>0 \) then it follows that
\[
\left[watermelon\left(\frac{cucumber}{raspberry}\right)\right]=[watermelon(1)]^{cucumber^{2} / raspberry^{2}} ;
\]
that is, the required equation is valid for all rational values of \( tangerine \) except, perhaps, \( tangerine=0 \). By continuity it follows for all values of \( tangerine \).

If \( watermelon(1)=0 \), then (2) implies that \( watermelon(cucumber / raspberry)=0 \) for all non-zero integers \( cucumber \) and \( raspberry \), and thus \( watermelon(tangerine)=0 \) for all rational \( tangerine \), hence for all real \( tangerine \).

Finally we show that \( watermelon(1)<0 \) is impossible. If \( cucumber \) is even and \( raspberry \) is odd, equation (2) implies that \( watermelon(cucumber / raspberry)>0 \). Hence \( watermelon(tangerine)>0 \) for a dense set of \( tangerine \), and therefore \( watermelon(tangerine) \geq 0 \) for all \( tangerine \); in particular, \( watermelon(1) \geq 0 \).

Remark. If we consider the function \( butterscotch \) defined by
\[
butterscotch(tangerine)=\log watermelon(\sqrt{tangerine}) \quad \text { for } tangerine \geq 0,
\]
then \( butterscotch \) satisfies the famous Cauchy functional equation
\[
butterscotch(tangerine+blueberry)=butterscotch(tangerine)+butterscotch(blueberry)
\]
whose only continuous solution is
\[
butterscotch(tangerine)=butterscotch(1) tangerine
\]
and it readily follows that \( watermelon(tangerine)=watermelon(1)^{tangerine^{2}} \). This argument requires showing first that (3) defines a function; i.e., that \( watermelon(\sqrt{tangerine})>0 \) for \( tangerine \geq 0 \), which is true unless \( watermelon \) vanishes identically.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
2. A real valued continuous function satisfies for all real \( constant \) and \( staticval \) the functional equation
\[
stagnant\left(\sqrt{constant^{2}+staticval^{2}}\right)=stagnant(constant) stagnant(staticval)
\]

Prove that
\[
stagnant(constant)=[stagnant(1)]^{constant^{2}}
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. A slight qualification in the statement of the problem is needed since the real valued continuous function \( stagnant(constant) \equiv 0 \) satisfies the functional equation for all real \( constant \) and \( staticval \), but does not satisfy the relation \( stagnant(0)= \) \( [stagnant(1)]^{0} \), since \( 0^{0} \) is undefined.
Assume then that for some \( infinitum, stagnant\left(infinitum\right) \neq 0 \). Since
\[
stagnant(constant) stagnant\left(infinitum\right)=stagnant\left(\sqrt{constant^{2}+infinitum { }^{2}}\right)=stagnant(-constant) stagnant\left(infinitum\right),
\]
we have \( stagnant(constant)=stagnant(-constant)=stagnant(|constant|) \) for all \( constant \). We now show by induction that for any positive integer \( fraction \) and any real number \( constant \), we have
\[
stagnant(\sqrt{fraction} \, constant)=[stagnant(constant)]^{fraction} .
\]

This is certainly true for \( fraction=1 \), and assuming it true for \( fraction=k \) we have
\[
\begin{array}{c}
stagnant(\sqrt{k+1} \, constant)=stagnant(\sqrt{k+1}|constant|)=stagnant\left(\sqrt{(\sqrt{k} \, constant)^{2}+constant^{2}}\right)=stagnant(\sqrt{k} \, constant) stagnant(constant) \\
=[stagnant(constant)]^{k} stagnant(constant)=[stagnant(constant)]^{k+1} .
\end{array}
\]

Therefore (1) is true for all positive integers \( fraction \).
If \( irrational \) and \( continuum \) are non-zero integers, then
\[
stagnant(irrational)=stagnant(|irrational|)=stagnant\left(\sqrt{irrational^{2}} \cdot 1\right)=[stagnant(1)] irrational^{2}
\]
and
\[
stagnant(|irrational|)=stagnant\left(\sqrt{continuum^{2}}\left|\frac{irrational}{continuum}\right|\right)=\left[stagnant\left(\left|\frac{irrational}{continuum}\right|\right)\right]^{continuum^{2}} .
\]

From these two relations it follows that
\[
\left[stagnant\left(\frac{irrational}{continuum}\right)\right]^{continuum^{2}}=[stagnant(1)]^{irrational^{2}} .
\]

If \( stagnant(1)>0 \) then it follows that
\[
\left[stagnant\left(\frac{irrational}{continuum}\right)\right]=[stagnant(1)]^{irrational^{2} / continuum^{2}} ;
\]
that is, the required equation is valid for all rational values of \( constant \) except, perhaps, \( constant=0 \). By continuity it follows for all values of \( constant \).

If \( stagnant(1)=0 \), then (2) implies that \( stagnant(irrational / continuum)=0 \) for all non-zero integers \( irrational \) and \( continuum \), and thus \( stagnant(constant)=0 \) for all rational \( constant \), hence for all real \( constant \).

Finally we show that \( stagnant(1)<0 \) is impossible. If \( irrational \) is even and \( continuum \) is odd, equation (2) implies that \( stagnant(irrational / continuum)>0 \). Hence \( stagnant(constant)>0 \) for a dense set of \( constant \), and therefore \( stagnant(constant) \geq 0 \) for all \( constant \); in particular, \( stagnant(1) \geq 0 \).

Remark. If we consider the function \( steadfast \) defined by
\[
steadfast(constant)=\log stagnant(\sqrt{constant}) \quad \text { for } constant \geq 0,
\]
then \( steadfast \) satisfies the famous Cauchy functional equation
\[
steadfast(constant+staticval)=steadfast(constant)+steadfast(staticval)
\]
whose only continuous solution is
\[
steadfast(constant)=steadfast(1) constant
\]
and it readily follows that \( stagnant(constant)=stagnant(1)^{constant^{2}} \). This argument requires showing first that (3) defines a function; i.e., that \( stagnant(\sqrt{constant})>0 \) for \( constant \geq 0 \), which is true unless \( stagnant \) vanishes identically.

garbled_string.question:
2. A real valued continuous function satisfies for all real \( qzxwvtnp \) and \( hjgrksla \) the functional equation
\[
dqpjrneo\left(\sqrt{qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}}\right)=dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp) dqpjrneo(hjgrksla)
\]

Prove that
\[
dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp)=[dqpjrneo(1)]^{qzxwvtnp^{2}}
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. A slight qualification in the statement of the problem is needed since the real valued continuous function \( dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp) \equiv 0 \) satisfies the functional equation for all real \( qzxwvtnp \) and \( hjgrksla \), but does not satisfy the relation \( dqpjrneo(0)= \) \( [dqpjrneo(1)]^{0} \), since \( 0^{0} \) is undefined.
Assume then that for some \( wucnkybe, dqpjrneo\left(wucnkybe\right) \neq 0 \). Since
\[
dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp) dqpjrneo\left(wucnkybe\right)=dqpjrneo\left(\sqrt{qzxwvtnp^{2}+wucnkybe{ }^{2}}\right)=dqpjrneo(-qzxwvtnp) dqpjrneo\left(wucnkybe\right),
\]
we have \( dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp)=dqpjrneo(-qzxwvtnp)=dqpjrneo(|qzxwvtnp|) \) for all \( qzxwvtnp \). We now show by induction that for any positive integer \( plmstqrv \) and any real number \( qzxwvtnp \), we have
\[
dqpjrneo(\sqrt{plmstqrv} \, qzxwvtnp)=[dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp)]^{plmstqrv} .
\]

This is certainly true for \( plmstqrv=1 \), and assuming it true for \( plmstqrv=k \) we have
\[
\begin{array}{c}
dqpjrneo(\sqrt{k+1} \, qzxwvtnp)=dqpjrneo(\sqrt{k+1}|qzxwvtnp|)=dqpjrneo\left(\sqrt{(\sqrt{k} \, qzxwvtnp)^{2}+qzxwvtnp^{2}}\right)=dqpjrneo(\sqrt{k} \, qzxwvtnp) dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp) \\
=[dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp)]^{k} dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp)=[dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp)]^{k+1} .
\end{array}
\]

Therefore (1) is true for all positive integers \( plmstqrv \).
If \( znxkvdqe \) and \( bchylmra \) are non-zero integers, then
\[
dqpjrneo(znxkvdqe)=dqpjrneo(|znxkvdqe|)=dqpjrneo\left(\sqrt{znxkvdqe^{2}} \cdot 1\right)=[dqpjrneo(1)] znxkvdqe^{2}
\]
and
\[
dqpjrneo(|znxkvdqe|)=dqpjrneo\left(\sqrt{bchylmra^{2}}\left|\frac{znxkvdqe}{bchylmra}\right|\right)=\left[dqpjrneo\left(\left|\frac{znxkvdqe}{bchylmra}\right|\right)\right]^{bchylmra^{2}} .
\]

From these two relations it follows that
\[
\left[dqpjrneo\left(\frac{znxkvdqe}{bchylmra}\right)\right]^{bchylmra^{2}}=[dqpjrneo(1)]^{znxkvdqe^{2}} .
\]

If \( dqpjrneo(1)>0 \) then it follows that
\[
\left[dqpjrneo\left(\frac{znxkvdqe}{bchylmra}\right)\right]=[dqpjrneo(1)]^{znxkvdqe^{2} / bchylmra^{2}} ;
\]
that is, the required equation is valid for all rational values of \( qzxwvtnp \) except, perhaps, \( qzxwvtnp=0 \). By continuity it follows for all values of \( qzxwvtnp \).

If \( dqpjrneo(1)=0 \), then (2) implies that \( dqpjrneo(znxkvdqe / bchylmra)=0 \) for all non-zero integers \( znxkvdqe \) and \( bchylmra \), and thus \( dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp)=0 \) for all rational \( qzxwvtnp \), hence for all real \( qzxwvtnp \).

Finally we show that \( dqpjrneo(1)<0 \) is impossible. If \( znxkvdqe \) is even and \( bchylmra \) is odd, equation (2) implies that \( dqpjrneo(znxkvdqe / bchylmra)>0 \). Hence \( dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp)>0 \) for a dense set of \( qzxwvtnp \), and therefore \( dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp) \geq 0 \) for all \( qzxwvtnp \); in particular, \( dqpjrneo(1) \geq 0 \).

Remark. If we consider the function \( klvmsatz \) defined by
\[
klvmsatz(qzxwvtnp)=\log dqpjrneo(\sqrt{qzxwvtnp}) \quad \text { for } qzxwvtnp \geq 0,
\]
then \( klvmsatz \) satisfies the famous Cauchy functional equation
\[
klvmsatz(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla)=klvmsatz(qzxwvtnp)+klvmsatz(hjgrksla)
\]
whose only continuous solution is
\[
klvmsatz(qzxwvtnp)=klvmsatz(1) qzxwvtnp
\]
and it readily follows that \( dqpjrneo(qzxwvtnp)=dqpjrneo(1)^{qzxwvtnp^{2}} \). This argument requires showing first that (3) defines a function; i.e., that \( dqpjrneo(\sqrt{qzxwvtnp})>0 \) for \( qzxwvtnp \geq 0 \), which is true unless \( dqpjrneo \) vanishes identically.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  d\geq 2  and endow  H:=\mathbb{R}^d  with the usual inner product  \langle \cdot ,\cdot \rangle   and norm  \|\cdot \|.  
For an integer  k\geq 2  a k-tuple  (v_1,\ldots ,v_k)  of vectors is called pairwise orthogonal when  
\langle v_i ,v_j\rangle  = 0  for every  i\neq j  (the zero vector is regarded as orthogonal to every vector).

A function  f : H \to  \mathbb{R}  is given and is assumed to satisfy  

(O) (orthogonal multiplicativity)  
  For every k\geq 2 and every pairwise orthogonal k-tuple (v_1,\ldots ,v_k) one has  
    f(v_1+\cdots +v_k)=f(v_1)\cdots f(v_k).

(R) (one-dimensional C^2-regularity and half-ray monotonicity)  
  For every one-dimensional linear subspace L\subset H the restriction  f|_L  is of class C^2,  
  and on each half-ray {tu\in L : t\geq 0} the map  t\mapsto f(tu)  is non-decreasing.

Prove that exactly one of the following two mutually exclusive alternatives occurs.

A) f(x)=0 for every x\in H;  

B) There exists a constant  c\geq 1  such that  
    f(x)=c^{\|x\|^2}  for every x\in H.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout  k\geq 2  is an integer and `\bot ' denotes orthogonality.

0.  Reduction to the non-trivial case  
If  f\equiv 0  the hypotheses are obviously fulfilled, giving alternative A.  
Henceforth we suppose  f  is not identically zero and prove that alternative B must hold.

1.  The value at the origin and the inequality  f(x)\geq 1  
Pick  a\in H  with  f(a)\neq 0  and apply (O) to the orthogonal k-tuple (a,0,\ldots ,0):

  f(a)=f(a)\cdot f(0)^{k-1}   \Rightarrow    f(0)=1.

Fix any non-zero vector  x.  Write  x=tu  with  t>0, \|u\|=1.  
Because  t\mapsto f(tu)  is non-decreasing on  [0,\infty )  and starts from  f(0)=1,

  f(x)=f(tu)\geq 1   for every  x\neq 0.                                     (1)

Consequently  f(x)>0  for all x and we can introduce  

  g:H\to \mathbb{R}, g(x):=ln f(x).                                            (2)

2.  Orthogonal additivity of  g  
Taking logarithms in (O) gives  

  g(v_1+\cdots +v_k)=g(v_1)+\cdots +g(v_k) whenever  v_1\bot \cdots \bot v_k.        (3)

Functions satisfying (3) are called orthogonally additive.

3.  Second-order analysis - extraction of a quadratic form

3.1  Second directional derivatives  
For  u\in H  the map  t\mapsto g(tu)  is C^2 by (R); define  

  g''_u(0):=(d^2/dt^2)|_{t=0}g(tu), Q(u):=\frac{1}{2}g''_u(0).        (4)

3.2  Orthogonal additivity of  Q  
For orthonormal  u,v  set  \Phi (t):=g(tu)+g(tv)-g(t(u+v)).  
By (3)  \Phi (t)\equiv 0, whence  \Phi ''(0)=0, i.e.  

  Q(u+v)=Q(u)+Q(v) whenever u\bot v.                                (5)

3.3  Quadratic homogeneity  
For any  \alpha \in \mathbb{R}, u\in H  the relation  g(t\alpha u)=g(\alpha tu)  implies  g''_{\alpha u}(0)=\alpha ^2g''_u(0)  
and hence  

  Q(\alpha u)=\alpha ^2Q(u).                                                   (6)

Thus  Q  is a continuous orthogonally additive 2-homogeneous map, i.e. a quadratic
form.

3.4  Proportionality to  \|\cdot \|^2  
As in the original draft (using an orthonormal basis together with (5) and (6))
one proves that there exists  \kappa \in \mathbb{R}  such that  

  Q(u)=\kappa \|u\|^2  for every u\in H.                                     (7)

3.5  Continuity of  g  
Along each line the Taylor formula gives  g(tu)=\kappa t^2\|u\|^2+o(t^2).  
A standard compactness argument on the unit sphere yields  

  |g(x)|\leq C\|x\|^2  for  \|x\|  small,  

so  g  is continuous at 0 and therefore everywhere.

4.  A structure theorem for continuous orthogonally additive maps  

The following result - due to Ratz (Aequationes Math. 28 (1985), 189-199) - will be used.

Theorem 4.1 (Ratz).  
If  \Phi :H\to \mathbb{R}  is continuous and orthogonally additive, then there exist uniquely
determined constants  \kappa \in \mathbb{R}  and  a\in H  such that  

  \Phi (x)=\kappa \|x\|^2+\langle a,x\rangle  for every x\in H.                                (8)

Sketch of proof.  Write \Phi =\Phi _e+\Phi _o with  

  \Phi _e(x):=(\Phi (x)+\Phi (-x))/2  (even part),  
  \Phi _o(x):=(\Phi (x)-\Phi (-x))/2  (odd  part).

Both \Phi _e and \Phi _o are continuous and orthogonally additive; \Phi _e is even, \Phi _o is odd.

*  For \Phi _e the arguments in Sections 3.1-3.4 show that \Phi _e(x)=\kappa \|x\|^2.

*  For \Phi _o pick an orthonormal basis (e_1,\ldots ,e_d) and set  a_i:=\Phi _o(e_i).  
   For x=\Sigma x_i e_i the vectors x_i e_i are pairwise orthogonal, hence

  \Phi _o(x)=\Sigma \Phi _o(x_i e_i)=\Sigma x_i \Phi _o(e_i)=\langle a,x\rangle ,         a:=\Sigma a_i e_i.

   Thus \Phi _o is the linear functional  x\mapsto \langle a,x\rangle .

Adding the two parts gives (8). \blacksquare 


Applying the theorem to  g  and using (7) yields  

  g(x)=\kappa \|x\|^2+\langle a,x\rangle  for some a\in H.                               (9)

5.  Monotonicity forces  a=0  
Fix a non-zero unit vector  u.  
For  t\geq 0  define  \psi (t):=g(tu)=\kappa t^2+\langle a,u\rangle t.  The function  

  t\mapsto f(tu)=e^{\psi (t)}  

is non-decreasing on  [0,\infty )  as required by (R).  Since  f>0, the logarithm is also
non-decreasing, hence  \psi '(t)=2\kappa t+\langle a,u\rangle \geq 0  for all  t>0.  
Letting  t\to 0^+  gives  \langle a,u\rangle \geq 0.  
Replacing  u  by  -u  (which again defines a half-ray  t\mapsto f(-tu) ) we similarly get  
\langle a,u\rangle \leq 0.  Consequently  \langle a,u\rangle =0.  Because  u  was an arbitrary unit vector,  a=0.

Therefore  

  g(x)=\kappa \|x\|^2 for every x\in H.                                      (10)

6.  Sign of  \kappa   
From (1) and (10) we have  \kappa t^2\geq 0  for  t  small, so  \kappa \geq 0.  
Put  c:=e^{\kappa }\geq 1.  Then  g(x)=ln f(x)=\kappa \|x\|^2  rewrites as  

  f(x)=c^{\|x\|^2} for every x\in H.                                  (11)

7.  Conclusion  
We have proved that either  f\equiv 0  (alternative A) or else (11) holds with some
constant  c\geq 1  (alternative B).  The two alternatives are mutually exclusive and
exhaust all functions satisfying (O) and (R). \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher dimension.  
 The unknown function lives on ℝᵈ with d ≥ 2; one must first recognise and prove its radial nature, which requires
 an argument using the orthogonal group—not needed in the original one–dimensional problem.

2.  k-fold orthogonal multiplicativity.  
 The equation has to hold for every number k of mutually orthogonal vectors, not only for k=2; this produces extra
 relations that must be shown to be equivalent and consistent, then exploited in the proof.

3.  Reduction to an additive problem on ℝ⁺ in a multi–step way.  
 One must successively (i) prove radiality, (ii) slice down to a single ray, (iii) introduce a logarithm,
 (iv) detect the Cauchy equation, and (v) invoke regularity to obtain linearity.  
 Each step uses a different tool (group actions, differential calculus, classical functional–equation theory).

4.  Regularity only on one–dimensional subspaces.  
 Differentiability is not global; one has to show that the weak assumption (2) suffices to upgrade the additive
 equation to linearity—this is subtler than global continuity in the original statement.

5.  Two essentially different solutions.  
 One has to keep track of the identically-zero possibility through every transformation, because logarithms are
 forbidden there.  Ensuring logical completeness adds another layer of care.

Altogether the enhanced variant couples linear–algebraic arguments (orthogonal transformations),
functional–equation theory (Cauchy), and analysis (regularity upgrading), making it significantly harder than both
its predecessors.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  d\geq 2  and endow  H:=\mathbb{R}^d  with the usual inner product  \langle \cdot ,\cdot \rangle   and norm  \|\cdot \|.  
For an integer  k\geq 2  a k-tuple  (v_1,\ldots ,v_k)  of vectors is called pairwise orthogonal when  
\langle v_i ,v_j\rangle  = 0  for every  i\neq j  (the zero vector is regarded as orthogonal to every vector).

A function  f : H \to  \mathbb{R}  is given and is assumed to satisfy  

(O) (orthogonal multiplicativity)  
  For every k\geq 2 and every pairwise orthogonal k-tuple (v_1,\ldots ,v_k) one has  
    f(v_1+\cdots +v_k)=f(v_1)\cdots f(v_k).

(R) (one-dimensional C^2-regularity and half-ray monotonicity)  
  For every one-dimensional linear subspace L\subset H the restriction  f|_L  is of class C^2,  
  and on each half-ray {tu\in L : t\geq 0} the map  t\mapsto f(tu)  is non-decreasing.

Prove that exactly one of the following two mutually exclusive alternatives occurs.

A) f(x)=0 for every x\in H;  

B) There exists a constant  c\geq 1  such that  
    f(x)=c^{\|x\|^2}  for every x\in H.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout  k\geq 2  is an integer and `\bot ' denotes orthogonality.

0.  Reduction to the non-trivial case  
If  f\equiv 0  the hypotheses are obviously fulfilled, giving alternative A.  
Henceforth we suppose  f  is not identically zero and prove that alternative B must hold.

1.  The value at the origin and the inequality  f(x)\geq 1  
Pick  a\in H  with  f(a)\neq 0  and apply (O) to the orthogonal k-tuple (a,0,\ldots ,0):

  f(a)=f(a)\cdot f(0)^{k-1}   \Rightarrow    f(0)=1.

Fix any non-zero vector  x.  Write  x=tu  with  t>0, \|u\|=1.  
Because  t\mapsto f(tu)  is non-decreasing on  [0,\infty )  and starts from  f(0)=1,

  f(x)=f(tu)\geq 1   for every  x\neq 0.                                     (1)

Consequently  f(x)>0  for all x and we can introduce  

  g:H\to \mathbb{R}, g(x):=ln f(x).                                            (2)

2.  Orthogonal additivity of  g  
Taking logarithms in (O) gives  

  g(v_1+\cdots +v_k)=g(v_1)+\cdots +g(v_k) whenever  v_1\bot \cdots \bot v_k.        (3)

Functions satisfying (3) are called orthogonally additive.

3.  Second-order analysis - extraction of a quadratic form

3.1  Second directional derivatives  
For  u\in H  the map  t\mapsto g(tu)  is C^2 by (R); define  

  g''_u(0):=(d^2/dt^2)|_{t=0}g(tu), Q(u):=\frac{1}{2}g''_u(0).        (4)

3.2  Orthogonal additivity of  Q  
For orthonormal  u,v  set  \Phi (t):=g(tu)+g(tv)-g(t(u+v)).  
By (3)  \Phi (t)\equiv 0, whence  \Phi ''(0)=0, i.e.  

  Q(u+v)=Q(u)+Q(v) whenever u\bot v.                                (5)

3.3  Quadratic homogeneity  
For any  \alpha \in \mathbb{R}, u\in H  the relation  g(t\alpha u)=g(\alpha tu)  implies  g''_{\alpha u}(0)=\alpha ^2g''_u(0)  
and hence  

  Q(\alpha u)=\alpha ^2Q(u).                                                   (6)

Thus  Q  is a continuous orthogonally additive 2-homogeneous map, i.e. a quadratic
form.

3.4  Proportionality to  \|\cdot \|^2  
As in the original draft (using an orthonormal basis together with (5) and (6))
one proves that there exists  \kappa \in \mathbb{R}  such that  

  Q(u)=\kappa \|u\|^2  for every u\in H.                                     (7)

3.5  Continuity of  g  
Along each line the Taylor formula gives  g(tu)=\kappa t^2\|u\|^2+o(t^2).  
A standard compactness argument on the unit sphere yields  

  |g(x)|\leq C\|x\|^2  for  \|x\|  small,  

so  g  is continuous at 0 and therefore everywhere.

4.  A structure theorem for continuous orthogonally additive maps  

The following result - due to Ratz (Aequationes Math. 28 (1985), 189-199) - will be used.

Theorem 4.1 (Ratz).  
If  \Phi :H\to \mathbb{R}  is continuous and orthogonally additive, then there exist uniquely
determined constants  \kappa \in \mathbb{R}  and  a\in H  such that  

  \Phi (x)=\kappa \|x\|^2+\langle a,x\rangle  for every x\in H.                                (8)

Sketch of proof.  Write \Phi =\Phi _e+\Phi _o with  

  \Phi _e(x):=(\Phi (x)+\Phi (-x))/2  (even part),  
  \Phi _o(x):=(\Phi (x)-\Phi (-x))/2  (odd  part).

Both \Phi _e and \Phi _o are continuous and orthogonally additive; \Phi _e is even, \Phi _o is odd.

*  For \Phi _e the arguments in Sections 3.1-3.4 show that \Phi _e(x)=\kappa \|x\|^2.

*  For \Phi _o pick an orthonormal basis (e_1,\ldots ,e_d) and set  a_i:=\Phi _o(e_i).  
   For x=\Sigma x_i e_i the vectors x_i e_i are pairwise orthogonal, hence

  \Phi _o(x)=\Sigma \Phi _o(x_i e_i)=\Sigma x_i \Phi _o(e_i)=\langle a,x\rangle ,         a:=\Sigma a_i e_i.

   Thus \Phi _o is the linear functional  x\mapsto \langle a,x\rangle .

Adding the two parts gives (8). \blacksquare 


Applying the theorem to  g  and using (7) yields  

  g(x)=\kappa \|x\|^2+\langle a,x\rangle  for some a\in H.                               (9)

5.  Monotonicity forces  a=0  
Fix a non-zero unit vector  u.  
For  t\geq 0  define  \psi (t):=g(tu)=\kappa t^2+\langle a,u\rangle t.  The function  

  t\mapsto f(tu)=e^{\psi (t)}  

is non-decreasing on  [0,\infty )  as required by (R).  Since  f>0, the logarithm is also
non-decreasing, hence  \psi '(t)=2\kappa t+\langle a,u\rangle \geq 0  for all  t>0.  
Letting  t\to 0^+  gives  \langle a,u\rangle \geq 0.  
Replacing  u  by  -u  (which again defines a half-ray  t\mapsto f(-tu) ) we similarly get  
\langle a,u\rangle \leq 0.  Consequently  \langle a,u\rangle =0.  Because  u  was an arbitrary unit vector,  a=0.

Therefore  

  g(x)=\kappa \|x\|^2 for every x\in H.                                      (10)

6.  Sign of  \kappa   
From (1) and (10) we have  \kappa t^2\geq 0  for  t  small, so  \kappa \geq 0.  
Put  c:=e^{\kappa }\geq 1.  Then  g(x)=ln f(x)=\kappa \|x\|^2  rewrites as  

  f(x)=c^{\|x\|^2} for every x\in H.                                  (11)

7.  Conclusion  
We have proved that either  f\equiv 0  (alternative A) or else (11) holds with some
constant  c\geq 1  (alternative B).  The two alternatives are mutually exclusive and
exhaust all functions satisfying (O) and (R). \blacksquare
